title: Schroeder's Conjecture
statement_text: If $G$ is a graph of genus $g$, then $c(G) \leq g + 3$.
context_text: Schroeder conjectured in 2001 that the cop number of any genus-$g$ graph is at most $g+3$, meaning one additional cop per handle beyond the planar bound of 3. The conjecture holds for $g=0$ (planar, by Theorem 1) and $g=1$ (toroidal), but remains open for all $g \geq 3$.
paper_contribution: This survey reviews all progress toward Schroeder's conjecture, including the upper bounds $c(G) \leq \lfloor 3g/2 \rfloor + 3$ (Schroeder) and the improvement $c(G) \leq \lfloor 4g/3 \rfloor + D$ (Bowler, Erde, Lehner, Pitz), confirms the conjecture for $g=0$ and $g=1$, and discusses capture time implications.
notes: PDF source — math may be garbled; central conjecture of this survey
paper_title: Topological directions in Cops and Robbers

title: Andreae-Schroeder Conjecture
statement_text: Any toroidal graph has cop number at most $3$.
context_text: Andreae first asked whether toroidal graphs have cop number at most 3; this problem was referenced in Schroeder's paper. No example of a toroidal graph with cop number 4 is known, while the best known upper bound from Schroeder's theorem yields cop number at most 4 for toroidal graphs.
paper_contribution: The survey presents this as an open conjecture and notes that no counterexample is known; the best current upper bound gives cop number at most 4 for toroidal graphs.
paper_title: Topological directions in Cops and Robbers

title: Clarke-Fiorini-Joret-Theis Conjecture
statement_text: If $g$ is a positive integer, then $\tilde{c}(g) = c(\lfloor g/2 \rfloor)$.
context_text: Clarke, Fiorini, Joret, and Theis conjectured that the lower bound in their Theorem 14 is always tight, relating the maximum cop number $\tilde{c}(g)$ over all non-orientable genus $g$ graphs to the maximum cop number $c(\lfloor g/2 \rfloor)$ over orientable genus $\lfloor g/2 \rfloor$ graphs.
paper_contribution: The survey presents this conjecture alongside Theorem 14, which establishes $c(\lfloor g/2 \rfloor) \leq \tilde{c}(g) \leq \frac{3}{2}(g+1)$.
notes: PDF source — math may be garbled; $\tilde{c}(g)$ denotes the maximum cop number over all graphs of non-orientable genus $g$, and $c(g)$ denotes the maximum over orientable genus $g$
paper_title: Topological directions in Cops and Robbers

title: Smallest 3-cop-win planar graph conjecture
statement_text: The smallest order $3$-cop-win planar graph is the dodecahedron.
context_text: It has been proved that the smallest order 3-cop-win graph overall is the Petersen graph. It is conjectured that the smallest planar graph with cop number 3 is the dodecahedron, but this remains open.
attributed_to: reference [5] in the paper (exact authors not identified in extracted text)
paper_contribution: The survey mentions this conjecture as an open problem concerning small-order planar graphs with cop number 3.
notes: PDF source; attributed to reference [5] whose full citation is not visible in the provided extraction
paper_title: Topological directions in Cops and Robbers

title: Classification of planar graphs by cop number
statement_text: Classify which planar graphs have cop number $i$, for $1 \leq i \leq 3$.
context_text: By Theorem 1, every connected planar graph has cop number 1, 2, or 3. Cop-win graphs are precisely the dismantlable ones, which may eventually aid in classifying planar cop-win graphs, but a complete classification for all three cop number values remains open.
notes: PDF source; stated as open in survey prose
paper_title: Topological directions in Cops and Robbers

title: Classification of outerplanar graphs by cop number
statement_text: Classify which outerplanar graphs are $1$-cop-win and which are $2$-cop-win.
context_text: By Theorem 3, every outerplanar graph has cop number at most 2. The smallest outerplanar graph with cop number 2 is the four cycle, but a full classification of outerplanar graphs by cop number remains open.
paper_title: Topological directions in Cops and Robbers

title: Tightness of linear capture time bound for planar graphs
statement_text: Is the bound $\mathrm{capt}_3(G) \leq 2n$ tight for planar graphs of order $n$? Does there exist a planar graph $G$ of order $n$ with $\mathrm{capt}(G) > n$?
context_text: Theorem 5 establishes $\mathrm{capt}_3(G) \leq 2n$ for planar graphs of order $n$, improving the earlier $O(n^2)$ bound of Theorem 4. Whether this linear bound is tight is unknown; even finding a single planar graph with capture time exceeding $n$ remains open.
paper_title: Topological directions in Cops and Robbers

title: Capture time bounds for higher genus graphs
statement_text: What are the bounds on the capture time for graphs of higher genus? If Schroeder's conjecture holds and $\gamma(G) = g$, is it true that $\mathrm{capt}_k(G) = O(n^{g+4})$ for every $k \geq g+3$?
context_text: The paper identifies bounding the capture time of graphs embedded on surfaces as an interesting open question. If Schroeder's conjecture holds, giving $c(G) \leq g+3$ for genus-$g$ graphs, then a natural bound on $\mathrm{capt}_k(G)$ would follow for $k \geq g+3$.
paper_title: Topological directions in Cops and Robbers
